stem: Which address is a valid IPv6 link-local unicast address?
explain_short: Link-local addresses live in fe80::/10 (first hextet fe80–febf). FE80::1:4545:6578:ABC1 is the only choice in that range.

explain_lecture.quote: IPv6 LLAs are in the fe80::/10 range.

stem: Which of these addresses is the shortest abbreviation for the IP address: 3FFE:1044:0000:0000:00AB:0000:0000:0057?
explain_short: Drop leading zeros in each hextet AND replace the longest run of all-zero hextets with :: (only once). The two zero hextets between AB and 57 are the longer run.

explain_lecture.slide: IPv6 Address Representation — Rule 2 — Double Colon
explain_lecture.quote: A double colon (::) can replace any single, contiguous string of one or more 16-bit hextets consisting of all zeros.

stem: A network administrator has received the IPv6 prefix 2001:DB8::/48 for subnetting. Assuming the administrator does not subnet into the interface ID portion of the address space, how many subnets can the administrator create from the /48 prefix?
explain_short: Standard host prefix is /64. Subnet bits = 64 − 48 = 16. 2^16 = 65,536 subnets.

explain_lecture.slide: Subnet an IPv6 Network — IPv6 Subnetting Example
explain_lecture.quote: Given the 2001:db8:acad::/48 global routing prefix with a 16 bit subnet ID. Allows 65,536 /64 subnets

stem: Given IPv6 address prefix 2001:db8::/48, what will be the last subnet that is created if the subnet prefix is changed to /52?
explain_short: /52 borrows 4 bits → 16 subnets. The 4 bits sit at the top of the 4th hextet, so subnets step by 0x1000: …:0::/52, …:1000::/52, …, …:f000::/52.

stem: Consider the following range of addresses: 2001:0DB8:BC15:00A0:0000::, 2001:0DB8:BC15:00A1:0000::, 2001:0DB8:BC15:00A2:0000::, …, 2001:0DB8:BC15:00AF:0000::. The prefix-length for the range of addresses is _______ .
explain_short: All addresses share 2001:0DB8:BC15:00A as common prefix; only the last hex digit of the 4th hextet varies (0–F = 4 bits). Common bits = 64 − 4 = 60 → /60.

stem: What type of IPv6 address is FE80::1?
explain_short: FE80::/10 is the link-local prefix. Loopback is ::1, multicast is FF00::/8, GUA starts with 2 or 3.

explain_lecture.quote: IPv6 LLAs are in the fe80::/10 range.

stem: What is used in the EUI-64 process to create an IPv6 interface ID on an IPv6 enabled interface?
choices: the MAC address of the IPv6 enabled interface
an IPv6 address that is provided by a DHCPv6 server
an IPv4 address that is configured on the interface
explain_short: EUI-64 takes the 48-bit MAC, splits it, inserts FFFE in the middle, and flips the 7th bit (U/L) of the first byte to produce a 64-bit interface ID.

explain_lecture.slide: Dynamic Addressing for IPv6 GUAs — EUI-64 Process
explain_lecture.quote: A 16 bit value of fffe (in hexadecimal) is inserted into the middle of the 48-bit Ethernet MAC address of the client. The 7th bit of the client MAC address is reversed from binary 0 to 1.

stem: What is the prefix for the host address 2001:DB8:BC15:A:12AB::1/64?
explain_short: /64 → first 64 bits = first 4 hextets are the network prefix. So the prefix is 2001:DB8:BC15:A.

stem: An IPv6 enabled device sends a data packet with the destination address of FF02::1. What is the target of this packet?
choices: the one IPv6 device on the link that has been uniquely configured with this address
all IPv6 enabled devices on the local link or network
explain_short: FF02::1 is the all-nodes multicast address — every IPv6 device on the link processes it. FF02::2 is the all-routers group.

explain_lecture.slide: IPv6 Multicast Addresses — Well-Known IPv6 Multicast Addresses
explain_lecture.quote: ff02::1 All-nodes multicast group - This is a multicast group that all IPv6-enabled devices join. A packet sent to this group is received and processed by all IPv6 interfaces on the link or network.

stem: Which IPv6 prefix is reserved for communication between devices on the same link?
explain_short: FE80::/10 is the link-local prefix. Link-local addresses are required on every IPv6 interface and are never routed off the link.

explain_lecture.quote: An IPv6 link-local address (LLA) enables a device to communicate with other IPv6-enabled devices on the same link and only on that link (subnet).

stem: Which type of IPv6 address refers to any unicast address that is assigned to multiple hosts?
explain_short: Anycast: a single unicast address configured on multiple devices; routing delivers to the nearest one.

explain_lecture.slide: IPv6 Address Types — Unicast, Multicast, Anycast
explain_lecture.quote: Anycast – This is any IPv6 unicast address that can be assigned to multiple devices. A packet sent to an anycast address is routed to the nearest device having that address.

stem: What are two types of IPv6 unicast addresses? (Choose two.)
explain_short: IPv6 unicast subtypes include global unicast, link-local, unique local, and loopback (::1). Multicast and anycast are separate categories; IPv6 has no broadcast.

explain_lecture.slide: IPv6 Address Types — Types of IPv6 Unicast Addresses
explain_lecture.quote: Unlike IPv4 devices that have only a single address, IPv6 addresses typically have two unicast addresses: Global Unicast Address (GUA) – This is similar to a public IPv4 address. These are globally unique, internet-routable addresses. Link-local Address (LLA) - Required for every IPv6-enabled device and used to communicate with other devices on the same local link.

stem: Which service provides dynamic global IPv6 addressing to end devices without using a server that keeps a record of available IPv6 addresses?
explain_short: SLAAC lets a host build its own GUA from RA-supplied prefix + an interface ID (EUI-64 or random). No server keeps state.

explain_lecture.slide: Dynamic Addressing for IPv6 GUAs — Method 1: SLAAC
explain_lecture.quote: SLAAC allows a device to configure a GUA without the services of DHCPv6. Devices obtain the necessary information to configure a GUA from the ICMPv6 RA messages of the local router.

stem: Which protocol supports Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC) for dynamic assignment of IPv6 addresses to a host?
explain_short: SLAAC uses ICMPv6 RS/RA messages. There is no ARPv6 — Neighbor Discovery (ICMPv6) replaces ARP.

explain_lecture.slide: Dynamic Addressing for IPv6 GUAs — RS and RA Messages
explain_lecture.quote: Devices obtain GUA addresses dynamically through Internet Control Message Protocol version 6 (ICMPv6) messages.

stem: Which is the compressed format of the IPv6 address 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:a0b0:0008:0001?
explain_short: Drop leading zeros: 2001:db8:0:0:0:a0b0:8:1. Replace the run of three zero hextets with :: → 2001:db8::a0b0:8:1.

explain_lecture.quote: The first rule to help reduce the notation of IPv6 addresses is to omit any leading 0s (zeros).

stem: Which is the compressed format of the IPv6 address fe80:09ea:0000:2200:0000:0000:0fe0:0290?
explain_short: Drop leading zeros (09ea→9ea, 0fe0→fe0, 0290→290). The longest zero run is the two hextets at positions 5–6, replace them with ::.

explain_lecture.slide: IPv6 Address Representation — Rule 2 — Double Colon
explain_lecture.quote: A double colon (::) can replace any single, contiguous string of one or more 16-bit hextets consisting of all zeros.

stem: Which is the compressed format of the IPv6 address 2002:0042:0010:c400:0000:0000:0000:0909?
explain_short: Strip leading zeros (0042→42, 0010→10, 0909→909). Replace the three contiguous zero hextets with ::.

stem: Which is the compressed format of the IPv6 address 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0ab8:0001:0000:1000?
explain_short: Two zero runs exist (positions 3–4 and position 7). Per RFC 5952 you compress the LONGEST run; the first run (2 hextets) wins, so 2001:db8::ab8:1:0:1000.

explain_lecture.slide: IPv6 Address Representation — Rule 2 — Double Colon
explain_lecture.quote: Note: The double colon (::) can only be used once within an address, otherwise there would be more than one possible resulting address.

stem: Which is the compressed format of the IPv6 address 2002:0420:00c4:1008:0025:0190:0000:0990?
explain_short: Strip leading zeros from each hextet, then replace the single zero hextet at position 7 with ::.

stem: Which is the compressed format of the IPv6 address fe80:0000:0000:0000:0220:0b3f:f0e0:0029?
explain_short: Replace the three contiguous zero hextets after fe80 with :: and strip leading zeros from the remaining hextets.

stem: Type the prefix used by IPv6 link-local unicast addresses (in shortest form, e.g. xx::/yy).
explain_short: Link-local addresses are required on every IPv6 interface and live in FE80::/10 (range FE80:: to FEBF::).

explain_lecture.quote: IPv6 LLAs are in the fe80::/10 range.

stem: Type the IPv6 multicast address used to reach all routers on the link (well-known group).
explain_short: FF02::2 is the all-routers multicast group; FF02::1 is all-nodes.

explain_lecture.slide: IPv6 Multicast Addresses — Well-Known IPv6 Multicast Addresses
explain_lecture.quote: ff02::2 All-routers multicast group - This is a multicast group that all IPv6 routers join. A router becomes a member of this group when it is enabled as an IPv6 router with the ipv6 unicast-routing global configuration command.

stem: On router R1, configure the IPv6 global unicast address 2001:db8:acad:1::1 with prefix length 64 on the currently selected interface.
explain_short: Cisco IOS uses 'ipv6 address <addr>/<prefix>' on an interface. Almost identical to 'ip address' for IPv4 — just prefix-length notation instead of mask.

explain_lecture.slide: GUA and LLA Static Configuration — Static GUA Configuration on a Router
explain_lecture.quote: The command to configure an IPv6 GUA on an interface is: ipv6 address ipv6-address/prefix-length.

stem: What is the IPv6 loopback address (in shortest form)?
explain_short: ::1 (i.e. 0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1) is the IPv6 loopback — the equivalent of 127.0.0.1.

explain_lecture.slide: How a Host Routes — Host Forwarding Decision
explain_lecture.quote: A host can send packets to the following: Itself – 127.0.0.1 (IPv4), ::1 (IPv6)

stem: What is the recommended IPv6 prefix length for LANs and most other types of networks?
explain_short: IETF recommends /64 for end-host networks because SLAAC requires a 64-bit interface ID.

explain_lecture.slide: IPv6 Address Types — IPv6 Prefix Length
explain_lecture.quote: The IPv6 prefix length can range from 0 to 128. The recommended IPv6 prefix length for LANs and most other types of networks is /64.
